coredata.dc:title: Atmospheric pressure synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots for fabrication of BiOBr nanohybrids with enhanced visible-light photoactivity and photostability
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Yuanyuan
Liu, Qian
Jiang, Ding
Du, Xiaojiao
Qian, Jing
Mao, Hanping
Wang, Kun
coredata.dc:description: A facile bottom-up approach for producing nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was carried out by the carbonization of ammonium citrate under atmospheric pressure. And further, N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with N-GQDs uniformly distributed on BiOBr nanoplates were fabricated by a one-pot solvothermal method using the proposed N-GQDs as the precursor. As an excellent enhancer, the doped N-GQDs could improve the photoactivity and photostability of BiOBr nanoplates in the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. Moreover, the photoactivity of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids was influenced remarkably by the content of the doped N-GQDs, and the results indicated that 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids showed the optimal photoactivity. The N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids showed excellent performance in the photoelectrochemical detection of glutathione as well as photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B. And a possible mechanism of N-GQDs on the enhancement photoactivity and photostability of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids was also proposed. This gave insights into N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids and described a novel method to design and fabricate high photoactivity nanohybrids related to N-GQDs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enhanced PEC performance of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids for GSH detection
Enhanced photocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids for RhB degradation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Qing Lan and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors genuinely appreciate the financial support of this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175061 , 21375050 and 21405063 ), Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology (No. NZ201109 ), Project Funded by Qing Lan and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2014-37). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration for the synthesis process of N-GQDs and N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) TEM image of N-GQDs. Inset: HRTEM image of N-GQDs. (B) AFM image of the N-GQDs on a Si substrate (C) UV–vis and PL spectra of N-GQDs. Inset: Photographs of N-GQDs solution taken under visible-light (a) and 365 nm UV light (b). XPS spectra of the as-prepared N-GQDs: (D) survey, (E) C 1s, and (F) N 1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) TEM images of BiOBr and (B) N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. (C) HRTEM image of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. (D) The wide scan XPS spectrum for 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) XRD patterns of pristine BiOBr (a), 0.1 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (b), 0.5 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (c), 1 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (d), 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (e), 5 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (f), and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (g). (B) FT-IR spectra of pristine BiOBr (a), 0.1 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (b), 0.5 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (c), 1 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (d), 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (e), 5 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (f), 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (g), and N-GQDs (h). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Photocurrent responses and (B) EIS spectra of N-GQDs/ITO (a), 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr/ITO (b), and BiOBr/ITO (c) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). Inset: The equivalent circuit used for ﬁtting the impedance data, where R
s , R
ct , Z
W , and Q represents electrolyte solution resistance, charge transfer resistance, Warburg impedance, and constant phase elements, respectively. (C) The photocurrent response of BiOBr (a), 0.1 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (b), 0.5 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (c), 1 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (d), 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (e), 5 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (f), 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (g), and N-GQDs (h) in the absence and presence of 100 μM GSH. (D) Schematic diagram of the photoelectrochemical process for determination of GSH at the N-GQDs/BiOBr/ITO. (E) Photocurrent responses of 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr/ITO in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) in the presence of 0 (a), 5 (b), 10 (c), 50 (d), 80 (e), 100 (f), 200 (g), 400 (h), 600 (i), and 800 μM (j) GSH under visible-light excitation. (F) The calibration curve for GSH detection. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Comparison of photocatalytic activities of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with different contents of N-GQDs for the degradation of RhB. (B) Temporal UV–vis absorption spectral changes during the photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presence of 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. Inset: photographs of photocatalytic degradation of RhB solution under visible-light. (C) UV–vis DRS and (D) plot of (Ahυ)2 versus the energy (hυ) for the band gap energy of BiOBr (a) and 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The element contents of ammonium citrate and N-GQDs and contents of different nitrogen species in N-GQDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: N1: Pyridine N N2: Pyrrolic N N3: Graphitic N N4: N–H.

head.ce:title: Atmospheric pressure synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots for fabrication of BiOBr nanohybrids with enhanced visible-light photoactivity and photostability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile bottom-up approach for producing nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) was carried out by the carbonization of ammonium citrate under atmospheric pressure. And further, N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with N-GQDs uniformly distributed on BiOBr nanoplates were fabricated by a one-pot solvothermal method using the proposed N-GQDs as the precursor. As an excellent enhancer, the doped N-GQDs could improve the photoactivity and photostability of BiOBr nanoplates in the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. Moreover, the photoactivity of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids was influenced remarkably by the content of the doped N-GQDs, and the results indicated that 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids showed the optimal photoactivity. The N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids showed excellent performance in the photoelectrochemical detection of glutathione as well as photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B. And a possible mechanism of N-GQDs on the enhancement photoactivity and photostability of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids was also proposed. This gave insights into N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids and described a novel method to design and fabricate high photoactivity nanohybrids related to N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a class of one- or few-layered graphene sheets with lateral dimension smaller than 100 nm, have been envisioned to fuel the development of research in electrochemical biosensors, drug delivery, bioimaging, and energy conversion due to their unique quantum confinement and edge effects [1,2]. Recent studies have demonstrated that chemical doping, especially nitrogen-doping can effectively tune the intrinsic properties of carbon-based material including electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical features [3], which made it a potential candidate for various applications such as enhanced catalysis for energy conversion and storage [4], improved photoactivity in pollutant degradation [5,6], and amplified electrochemical signal in biosensing [7]. Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), a significant carbon nanomaterial doped with nitrogen atoms, have ignited tremendous interests due to their unique photochemistry properties, electrocatalytic activity, electrical conductivity combined with low toxicity and biocompatibility [8–10]. On account of these properties [11], N-GQDs have been attracting increasing interest in some fields such as oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [8,12], biomedical imaging [13] and environmental monitoring [14,15]. For instance, used N-GQDs as a metal-free electrocatalysts, Li et al. reported that the N-GQDs possessed an enhanced electrocatalytic activity for the ORR comparable to that of a commercially available Pt/C catalyst [8]. According to Ju's report, the N-GQDs could be used as a sensing platform for label-free sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution and real water samples due to the selective coordination to Fe3+ [14]. In our previous work, based on the N-GQDs decorated GO (N-GQDs-GO) with excellent ECL performance, an ultrasensitive assay for pentachlorophenol detection was constructed [15]. Most recently, Wang et al. coupled N-GQDs with TiO2 nanofibers to achieve visible-light-driven photocatalyst N-GQDs-sensitized TiO2 nanofibers, which exhibit higher photoelectrical activity than using pure TiO2 nanofibers as electrode material [16]. The interesting result indicated that N-GQDs could be used as an enhanced material for improving the photoelectrical performance of TiO2 nanofibers by N-GQDs sensitization, which opens up new prospects for the utilization of N-GQDs as a novel enhanced material in various applications and up to now, it is just the beginning of this fantastic topic.

Since the discovery of its photoactivity under UV- or visible-light illumination, semiconductor BiOBr has attracted considerable attention because of its suitable band gap, stability and relatively superior visible-light-induced photoactivity [17,18]. However, the band gap of BiOBr was reported to be around 2.8 eV, indicating that it cannot absorb a significant part of visible-light [19]. In order to utilize visible-light more efficiently and improve visible-light photoactivity of BiOBr, intensive efforts have been focused on combine BiOBr with other materials such as semiconductor [20–22], metal [23,24] and carbon-based materials (multiwalled carbon nanotubes [25] and graphene [26–28]) to form BiOBr-based nanocomposites. Such nanocomposites integrate the synergistic effects of the individual species, which could endow the composite systems with increased light harvesting, prolonged lifetime of carriers, as well as higher chemical stability [29]. For example, the BiOBr-g-C3N4 inorganic-organic composite were synthesized by a one-step chemical bath method according to Zan's report, which showed much higher visible-light-driven photocatalytic activity than pure g-C3N4 and BiOBr for rhodamine B (RhB) degradation [21]. Jiang et al. have prepared a highly active visible-light-induced photocatalyst based on BiOBr with Ti doping and Ag decorating, and the photocatalyst exhibits highest activity and excellent durability for the degradation of RhB [23]. Tu and co-workers have synthesized BiOBr/graphene photocatalyst through a facile solvothermal method, which demonstrated significant improvement over pure BiOBr in the photodegradation of RhB dye under visible-light irradiation [27]. All these results above indicated that the coupling of BiOBr with other materials can improve the intrinsic photoactivity of the pristine BiOBr effectively, which may pave the way for the practical applications of BiOBr-based nanocomposites in the fields of environmental pollutants detection and removal.

To date, some strategies have been proposed for the controllable preparation of N-GQDs such as electrochemical and solution chemistry approaches [10,30]. However, all these above-mentioned methods suffer from more or less drawbacks due to the expensive equipment/high pressure required, complicated and time-consuming procedures. In this work, a one-pot, environment-friendly bottom-up strategy was reported to large scale synthesis of N-GQDs by the carbonization of ammonium citrate (AC) under atmospheric pressure. Subsequently, used bismuth nitrate and N-GQDs as the precursors, a series of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with different N-GQDs contents were fabricated through a facile solvothermal route. It is worth noting that the proposed N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids exhibited enhanced photoactivity as well as photostability in comparison with pristine BiOBr nanoplates, which provided a fast and facile method for the preparation of N-GQDs related nanohybrids with excellent performance.

AC, cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), ethylene glycol (EG), Bi(NO3)3·5H2O, glutathione (GSH), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 0.1 M phosphate buffered solution (PBS, pH7.4) was used as the supporting electrolyte, which was prepared by mixing stock standard solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. Other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout the study.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurements were conducted using a JEOL 100 instrument (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were carried out using Bruker Innova Microscope instrument in tapping mode using antimony n-doped Si tip of 10 nm resolution. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were obtained by a Quanta Master & Time Master Spectrofluorometer with an excitation wavelength at 356 nm. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λex = 633 nm. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a VG MultiLab 2000 system with a monochromatic Mg-Kα source operated at 20 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted on a Bruker D8 diffractometer with high-intensity Cu Kα (λ = 1.54 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were obtained from Spectrometer (Nicolet Nexus 470 FT-IR). The photoelectrochemical (PEC) measurements were conducted using CHI660 B electrochemical analyzer (Chen Hua Instruments, Shanghai, China) with a conventional three-electrode system where a modiﬁed ITO electrode was employed as a working electrode, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) electrode as a reference electrode, and platinum wire as a counter electrode. All the PEC measurements were performed in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) at 0 V, and a 250 W Xe lamp (Beijing Trusttech Co. Ltd.) was used as the visible-light source with an intensity (passing through a 400 nm UV-cut filter) of 100 mW cm−2. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4− with a frequency range from 0.1Hz to 10 kHz at 0.25 V, and the amplitude of the applied sine wave potential in each case was 5 mV.

An easy reflux approach was used for the atmospheric pressure preparation of N-GQDs (Fig. 1
). Specifically, 0.5 g AC and 15 mL H2O were put into a three-necked flask and heated to 200 °C using an oil bath pan, and a reflux condenser was used to make the steam, generated under the high temperature, condensed into liquid during the experiment. A balloon was applied in the outlet of the condenser pipe (Fig. 1), which had two functions: the first was to maintain a relative closed environment to make N atoms doped into GQDs, the second was to verify that the experiment was carried out under atmospheric pressure. Subsequently, the color of the liquid was changed from colorless to pale yellow, and then orange in 0.5 h, suggesting the formation of N-GQDs. Ultimately, The pH of obtained aqueous solution was adjusted to 7.0 by the addition of NaOH solution (10 mg mL−1) and the N-GQDs solution was stored at 4 °C for later use.

The N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids were prepared by a one-pot solvothermal method by using the proposed N-GQDs above as the precursor, as shown in Fig. 1. In brief, 1.08 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added into 9 mL of EG containing stoichiometric amount of CTAB. The mixture was stirred for 0.5 h at room temperature, and then 10 mL water containing 10.2 mg of as-prepared N-GQDs was added into the suspension under magnetic stirring for 1 h. The resulting suspension was then transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The autoclave was allowed to be heated at 140 °C for 12 h under autogenous pressure and then air-cooled to room temperature. The resulting precipitates were collected and washed with ethanol and deionized for thrice, followed by the drying in oven at 50 °C to obtain 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. The preparation of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with different starting mass ratios of N-GQDs to BiOBr (0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, and 0.07) were in according with the above procedure and were labeled as 0.1 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, 0.5 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, 1 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, 5 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, and 7 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, respectively. Pristine BiOBr nanoplates were also prepared by the same method without the addition of N-GQDs.

Before the modification of ITO, 2 mg of sample was dispersed ultrasonically in 1 mL DMF to obtain 2 mg mL−1 suspension. The ITO electrodes were put into boiling water containing 0.1 M NaOH for 0.5 h, and then were ultrasonically cleaned in water and alcohol for 0.5 h, respectively. 25 μL of the resulting solution dispersion was drop-cast onto a piece of ITO slice with a fixed area of 1 cm2 and dried in air at room temperature to obtain N-GQDs/BiOBr modified ITO electrode (denoted as N-GQDs/BiOBr/ITO).

The photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples were evaluated by the degradation of RhB under a 300 W Xe lamp with a 400 nm cutoff filter. The photocatalytic experiments were carried out at 30 °C with a circulating water system to avoid thermal catalytic effects. Aeration was carried out to make sure the constant supply of oxygen and the complete mixing of the solution and photocatalyst during the photoreactions by means of using air pump. In a typical photocatalytic experiment, 0.03 g of photocatalyst was dispersed into 100 mL of the RhB (20 mg L−1) solution. Before irradiation, the suspension was magnetically stirred for 0.5 h in the dark to make sure that the RhB could reach the absorption–desorption equilibrium on the surface of the photocatalyst. At regular intervals, 4 mL suspension was sampled and analyzed with a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 553 nm to measure its absorbance.

The TEM image of the N-GQDs was shown in Fig. 2A. As can be seen, N-GQDs with 1–5 nm in size can be observed. The HRTEM result (the inset of Fig. 2A) indicated high crystallinity of the N-GQDs with lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, which was accordant with that of the graphite carbon source. The 2D features and thicknesses of the N-GQDs were further characterized by the corresponding AFM (Fig. 2B), which revealed an average typical topographic height of 3 nm, suggesting that most of the N-GQDs consisted of few layers.

UV–vis absorption and PL spectra were two types of effective techniques in the characterization of the as-prepared N-GQDs. Fig. 2C presented the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of N-GQDs. Obviously, the N-GQDs exhibited a typical absorption peak at 332 nm, which was corresponding with the previous report [14]. The inset in Fig. 2C showed bright blue fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm, while no fluorescence was observed under visible-light irradiation. Besides, the maximum excitation and minimum emission of N-GQDs spectra were at 357 nm and 433 nm, respectively. In addition, it was obvious that the N-GQDs showed emission at 433 nm, which was 18 nm blue-shifted compared with the GQDs (451 nm, synthesized by the same method using citric acid as raw materials) in Fig. S1. The result of the blue-shifted PL emission of N-GQDs was accordance with previous report [14] and it was believed to be from the strong electron affinity of N atoms doped in the N-GQDs [14,31]. All the results above demonstrated the successful preparation of N-GQDs.

XPS measurements were performed to examine the chemical composition of the as-synthesized N-GQDs. The survey spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. 2D) showed three predominant peaks at 284.0, 400.0, and 530.6 eV, which were corresponded to the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The element contents of ammonium citrate and N-GQDs showed in Table 1 indicated that the carbon content of N-GQDs was much higher than that of AC, implying that AC should be carbonized in some degree during the pyrolysis. Fig. 2E revealed the expanded XPS spectrum of the C1s peaks at 284.6, 285.2, 286.6, 287.3, and 289.0 eV, which can be assigned to C–C, C–N, C–O, CO, and O–CO, respectively [32–34]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum of the N-GQDs was showed in Fig. 2F, by means of N 1s XPS-peak-differentiation-imitating analyses, it is deduced that there are four kinds of N chemical forms in N-GQDs: pyridine-like N (398.1 eV), pyrrolic-like N (399.2 eV), graphitic-like N (400.5 eV) [35], and N–H (401.9 eV) [32], and the content of each types of N bonding was 0.08%, 1.02%, 0.93%, and 0.96% respectively (Table 1). The above XPS analysis clearly confirmed the successful adulteration of N atoms into the GQDs.

Fig. 3A showed TEM image of BiOBr. It could be clearly seen that the BiOBr nanoplates were irregular and overlapped with each other. The TEM and HRTEM images of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids were shown in Fig. 3B and C, respectively. By comparison with pristine BiOBr nanoplates, the 2D BiOBr nanoplates were decorated uniformly with N-GQDs in an average diameter of 1–5 nm (Fig. 3B), which were in close contact with BiOBr. Moreover, the HRTEM image (Fig. 3C) showed that N-GQDs were actually existed on the surface of BiOBr nanoplates. The lattice spacing of 0.277 nm of the nanoplates was ascribed to the (110) lattice plane of tetragonal BiOBr and the N-GQDs with lattice spacing of 0.20 nm were observed. Thus, it was reasonable to conclude that N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids were obtained successfully by a simple solvothermal method.

The Raman absorption spectra of the N-GQDs/BiOBr and BiOBr were illustrated in Fig. S2. Compared with pure BiOBr, the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids displayed two relatively obvious peaks at 1385 and 1580 cm−1, corresponding to the D band and G band of N-GQDs, respectively [8]. However, the characteristic peak of the N-GQDs failed to be observed (curve a) due to the fact that the characteristics of the Raman signal was covered by the intense fluorescence of the N-GQDs [32,35].

The XPS spectra were carried out to study the chemical composition of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 3D) clearly displayed the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids were composed of Bi, O, Br, and C elements, respectively. However, no signal of N element can be found in the survey XPS spectrum because of the low amount of N element compared with other elements. Fig. S3A showed two strong peaks at 158.7 and 164.5 eV and they were assigned to Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, which were characteristic of Bi3+ in the BiOBr nanoplates [36]. The Br 3d peak located at 68.20 eV (Fig. S3B) [37], which was characteristic of Br− in the BiOBr nanoplates. Furthermore, the high resolution of O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S3C) could be associated with three peaks at binding energies of 530.0, 532.1, and 533.0 eV, respectively, which were characteristic of oxygen in N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. Fig. S3D showed the C1s XPS spectra of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. The peak located at 284.7 eV was ascribed to surface adventitious carbon arisen from N-GQDs. Based on the above result, it can be further confirmed that the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids have been successfully synthesized.

The XRD analysis was carried to obtain the phase and structural information of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with different contents of N-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4A, it can be clearly seen that all the diffraction peaks can be indexed to the tetragonal phase of BiOBr (JCPDS No. 73-2061) with well-resolved (011), (012), (110), (112), (020), (014), (211), (212), (220), (124), and (032) reflections [18,37,38]. Meanwhile, no diffraction peaks corresponding to N-GQDs were observed in N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids, which was due to a little amount of N-GQDs that cannot be resolved by XRD. In addition, no other diffraction peaks can be observed, suggesting the high-purity of obtained samples without formation of impurities.

To further conﬁrm the formation of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids, the FT-IR spectra of BiOBr, N-GQDs, and N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with different contents of N-GQDs were showed in Fig. 4B. The peak located at 3511 cm−1 was related to O–H stretching, which was ascribed to the water adsorbed [39]. The FT-IR spectra of containing BiOBr (curve a-g) exhibited peaks at 510 cm−1, which were related to the Bi–O stretching vibration. As for containing N-GQDs (curve b-h), it was clear that the stretching vibration of N–H at 1586 cm−1 and C–N at 1400 cm−1 can be observed, which may correspond to the successful adulteration of N atom. Besides, the absorption band at 1078 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O. After the BiOBr was combined with N-GQDs (curve b-g), all above absorption bands can be observed in N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids, which indicated that BiOBr and N-GQDs have been coupled together successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced PEC performance of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids for GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: The PEC performance of N-GQDs, 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, and BiOBr modified electrode were investigated in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) under visible-light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5A, the photocurrent of the 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr/ITO (curve b) was about 20 times and 3.3 times higher than that of N-GQDs/ITO (curve a) and BiOBr/ITO (curve c), respectively. Moreover, the photocurrent of 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr/ITO exhibited more stable than that of N-GQDs/ITO and BiOBr/ITO, which was attributed to the introduction of N-GQDs in the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. N-GQDs possessed good electrical conductivity which could accelerate the electron transfer [40,41]. In addition, when N-GQDs were doped into BiOBr, the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron and hole pairs was increased because of excellent electron transfer ability of N-GQDs. Therefore, N-GQDs played a significant role in enhancing and stabilizing the PEC activity of BiOBr.

To further study electron transfer rate of as-prepared samples, the EIS of N-GQDs, 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, and BiOBr were investigated by using [Fe(CN)6]3–/4– as the redox probe. As shown in Fig. 5B, it can be found that the electron-transfer resistance (R ct) value of N-GQDs, 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr, and BiOBr were about 80, 120, and 165 Ω, respectively. The result indicated that the coupling of N-GQDs with BiOBr was beneficial for enhancing electron transport ability of the pristine BiOBr nanoplates. Besides, a smaller R ct of the 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr indicated that more effective separation of photo-generated electron–hole pairs and faster interfacial charge transfer had occurred.

Based on their good charge-carrier-transfer behavior and enhanced photocurrent intensity, the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids were considered to be an ideal candidate material for PEC application in the field of biological detection. The PEC performance of the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids had been investigated through the sensing of GSH, one of the most important molecules in human's metabolism [42,43]. The PEC behaviors of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with different contents of N-GQDs modified ITO in the absence and presence of GSH were investigated (Fig. 5C). It was found that the photocurrent of the samples modified ITO was obviously enhanced after the addition of GSH. Furthermore, among all N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with different contents of N-GQDs modified ITO, 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr/ITO not only exhibited the optimal photocurrent, but also showed the maximum increment of the photocurrent, indicating that 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr/ITO had the optimal sensitive properties in the PEC sensing of GSH.

The reason why the photocurrent was enhanced with the addition of GSH under visible-light irradiation is worth studying. As shown in Fig. 5D, the BiOBr nanoplates absorbed visible-light and induced electrons transformed from valance band (VB) to conduction band (CB), meanwhile, holes were generated in the VB. Photogenerated electron–hole pairs were thus obtained [44]. In the presence of N-GQDs, the electrons transferred from BiOBr nanoplates to N-GQDs, and then to the ITO to generate photocurrent. Due to the excellent electrical conductivity of N-GQDs in the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids, the N-GQDs could accelerate the electron transfer, consequently reducing the recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons and holes. Thus, the PEC activity of the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids was consequently enhanced. When the GSH existed, it was oxidized into glutathione (GSSG) by the positive charged holes in the solution. As a result, the recombination of electron–hole pairs could be effectively avoid due to the consumption of holes by the GSH, leading to enhanced photocurrent intensity [45].

On account of the excellent photoelectrochemical behavior, the 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids were selected as the optimal sensing interface and applied for the PEC detection of GSH. Fig. 5E demonstrated the photocurrent response in the presence of different concentration of GSH with 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr/ITO at an applied potential of 0 V with visible-light irradiation. As can be seen in Fig. 5E, the photocurrents increased gradually with the concentration of GSH increased. Under the optimal condition, a good linear relationship was obtained between the photocurrent intensity (I) and the GSH concentration (Fig. 5F). The linear equation was I = 0.0014 (c/μM) + 0.4158 (R
2 = 0.993) over a concentration range from 5 to 800 μM. The detection limit was 1.7 μM (S/N = 3). In addition, this work showed wider liner response with lower detection limit for GSH detection than other strategies in previous reports [42,43].

The selectivity of the developed PEC sensor was also evaluated against other coexisting species. Under the optimum conditions, the inﬂuence of various substances acted as potentially interfering compounds on the determination of GSH was studied with 0.1 mM GSH in PBS (pH 7.4) (Fig. S4). The result indicated that 1000-fold mass ratio of glucose, 300-fold mass ratio of glycine, glutamic, ascorbic acid, dopamine, cysteine, 800-fold mass ratio of Na+, K+, and Ca2+ had no influence on the determination of GSH. Therefore, the PEC sensor exhibited excellent speciﬁcity for the detection of GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced photocatalytic activity of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids for RhB degradation
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the samples for the degradation of RhB in water was tested under visible-light irradiation. Fig. 6A exhibited the photocatalytic activities of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with different contents of N-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 6A, all the samples showed the photocatalytic activities towards the degradation of RhB. Among all the samples, it was obviously that 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids exhibited optimal visible-light photocatalytic activity, which was corresponding with the previous PEC behavior. This result showed that the appropriate introduction of N-GQDs could enhance the separation efficiency of electrons and holes, making improvement of photocatalytic performance with pristine BiOBr. Fig. 6B showed the time-dependent absorption spectra of RhB solution in the presence of 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. As can be seen in Fig. 6B, the maximum absorption peak of RhB solution changed from 553 to 500 nm. Moreover, its color changed gradually from fuchsia to colorless (inset of Fig. 6B) and the degradation efficiency reached 98% after 1 h irradiation which indicated superior photocatalytic properties of the 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. The cycling experiment for the photodegradation of RhB in the presence of 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids was also carried out and the result was shown in Fig. S5, which indicated that after 5 cycles, the photocatalytic activity of the 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids almost unchanged, indicating the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids can be applied in the field of photochemical catalysis.

In order to investigate the optical properties of samples, the as-prepared N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids were measured using a UV–vis DRS in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Fig. 6C showed the UV–vis DRS of pristine BiOBr nanoplates and 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. Compared with the pristine BiOBr nanoplates, the reflectance of 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids within the visible-light range apparently increased and showed a red shift, which was due to the introduction of N-GQDs and the coupling together of the two materials. As a result, the light absorption efficiency of 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids was enhanced, which was beneficial for the photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the band gap energy (E
g ) of semiconductor material could be calculated from the following equation [46]: αE photon = K(E photon − E g)n/2, where α was absorption coefficient, E photon was the discrete photoenergy, K was a constant for the material, and n was 2 for a direct transition or 4 for an indirect transition [47]. Fig. 6D displayed plots for the relationship of (αE photon)1/2 versus E photon, which indicated that the bandgap of 3 wt% N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids was calculated to be 2.44 eV, lower than that of pristine BiOBr (2.63 eV). A lower band gap was beneficial for the electronic transitions and could excite generation of active species more easily [48].

In summary, a facile and green method was developed for the successful preparation of N-GQDs by the carbonization of low toxicity ammonium citrate under atmospheric pressure. And the N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids with enhanced visible-light photoactivity and photostability were synthesized by a simple solvothermal method using N-GQDs as the precursor. The amount of the N-GQDs had a significant influence on the photoactivity of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids, and the optimal starting mass ratio of N-GQDs to BiOBr was 0.03. The enhanced photoactivity of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids could be attributed to the excellent conductivity of N-GQDs and the effective light absorption of N-GQDs/BiOBr nanohybrids. This work showed a promising approach in the fabrication of N-GQDs related nanohybrids with preferable PEC and photocatalytic performance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors genuinely appreciate the financial support of this work from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175061, 21375050 and 21405063), Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology (No. NZ201109), Project Funded by Qing Lan and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2014-37).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.10.068.
